key_insight: Adding plasticity, damage, and particle-based fragmentation modeling extends graphite runaway-electron response predictions beyond failure onset to the full explosive phase.
engineering_relevance: The model improves confidence in predicting RE-induced material damage in fusion devices and provides a pathway toward quantitatively assessing more critical plasma-facing materials.
method: Use Johnson-Holmquist constitutive modeling to capture nonlinear plastic damage in graphite.
Couple finite element analysis with smoothed-particle hydrodynamics to represent fragmentation and debris expulsion.
Apply an effective plastic strain-based failure criterion instead of a purely strength-based onset criterion.
evidence_signal: The extended thermomechanical model was benchmarked successfully against two controlled runaway-electron damage experiments in DIII-D.
watch_item: The approach is still a constitutive modeling framework for controlled graphite cases, so transferability to other materials or fully reactor-relevant conditions needs further validation.
paper_title: Runaway electron induced explosions of graphite; modeling versus controlled DIII-D experiments
